name: J. Mayo Williams
description: American football player and coach, music producer (1894–1980)
abstract: Jay Mayo "Ink" Williams (September 25, 1894 – January 2, 1980) was a pioneering African-American producer of recorded blues music. Some historians have claimed that Ink Williams earned his nickname by his ability to get the signatures of talented African-American musicians on recording contracts, but in fact it was a racial sobriquet fro…

about_quotes.speaker: Sammy Price
about_quotes.evidence: after meeting Mr. J. Mayo Williams, who was A&R man for Brunswick, I got a chance to work for him in November, 1929. At this time I recorded my first record...

about_quotes.speaker: Mrs. Francis Oliver
about_quotes.evidence: Ink Williams, he sent Ink to take me out one day and tried to show me all the interesting places in Chicago to museums and things like that.

about_quotes.speaker: Buddy Burton
about_quotes.evidence: Jell-O'-Roll got me to make a couple of records with him... which we always called him Ink Williams.

about_quotes.speaker: Alberta Hunter
about_quotes.evidence: He was the -- I think he's the one that first came into Dreamland and spoke to me about recording.

about_quotes.speaker: Dorothy Donegan
about_quotes.evidence: How did you meet J. Mayo Williams? I don’t know. I guess he met me first. “I want you to record.”
about_quotes.doc_title: Dorothy Donegan — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History
